title: Rung-3 dispatch of runVerifySpot is not wrapped — unhandled throw escapes MCP boundary
description: After snapshot+overwrite+saveState succeed in rung-3, requestHint dispatches `runVerifySpot({ projectRoot }, opts)` with no surrounding try/catch. runVerifySpot deliberately re-throws any verification error that is not a VerificationModeUnsupportedError (verifySpot.ts line 87 `throw err`), so an unexpected exception from runVerification (e.g. a spawnFn that rejects synchronously with an unusual error class, or a future verification adapter that throws something other than VerificationModeUnsupportedError) propagates as a rejected promise out of runRequestHint. The MCP transport then reports a generic transport error rather than a structured `{ ok: false, error: { kind: 'auto-write-failed' | … } }` response, even though the snapshot, target_file overwrite, and state mutation have already been committed.
impact: Caller loses the structured failure shape that the contract promises (A2 says every documented branch returns `{ ok: false, error: { kind } }`). The student/agent never learns the auto-write committed; on retry the rotation logic will overwrite the only recoverable snapshot. Defense-in-depth that M001 added at verifySpot.ts is bypassed here because requestHint is the OUTER boundary on the rung-3 path.
recommendation: Wrap the runVerifySpot dispatch in try/catch; on rejection, return `{ ok: true, payload, newLadder, autoVerifyResult: { pass: false, advanced: false, output: <error message> } }` (or a new `auto-verify-failed` error kind) so callers see a structured response and the auto_completed=true persistence is acknowledged.
evidence:
// (e) Dispatch runVerifySpot to auto-verify the written file
  const verifyResult = await runVerifySpot({ projectRoot }, opts);

title: verifySpot pass-but-saveState-failed `error` field is silently dropped on rung-3 path
description: When the dispatched runVerifySpot returns `{ pass: true, advanced: false, error: 'verification passed but state persist failed: …' }` (the M002 carry-forward branch in verifySpot.ts lines 96-103), requestHint's autoVerifyResult only forwards `pass`, `advanced`, `output`. The `error` string is discarded. AutoVerifyResult does not even declare an `error` field. The student sees `autoVerifyResult: { pass: true, advanced: false }` with no explanation that the cursor failed to advance because state.json could not be persisted.
impact: Silent partial failure on a load-bearing path. A successful verification that fails to commit advancement looks identical to a normal pass-without-advance, which is misleading. The student/agent will wonder why the cursor did not move and may retry verification, masking the underlying disk error (EACCES/ENOSPC on .sui-deepbook-course).
recommendation: Add an optional `error?: string` to AutoVerifyResult and forward `verifyResult.error` into it. Alternatively surface the verifySpot persist failure as a top-level `state-save-failed` error on the rung-3 response, since the cycle as a whole did not durably persist the cursor advance.

title: Rung-3 saveState failure leaves auto_completed un-persisted while side effects are committed — violates AC-5.4
description: On rung-3, runAutoWrite has already snapshotted the .bak and overwritten target_file when saveState is called. If saveState rejects (EACCES/ENOSPC), requestHint returns `{ ok: false, error: { kind: 'state-save-failed', … } }` and exits — but on disk: (a) target_file is permanently overwritten, (b) `.bak` exists with the original, (c) state.json's ladder still records `auto_completed: false`. The contract Behavior section explicitly says "auto_completed is set as soon as the rung-3 read+write+flip atomic block completes — the only way to 'not set' it is for the read or snapshot or overwrite to fail before the flag flip". Here the flip succeeded in memory, but disk does not reflect it. AC-5.4 is therefore violated under saveState failure.
impact: A retry of `requestHint({ rung: 3 })` is allowed (gating only checks reference_shown). The retry will rotate the existing `.bak` (containing the ORIGINAL bytes) to `.bak.<ts>` and snapshot the now-overwritten target_file as the new `.bak`. The original is still recoverable but only via the rotated copy. More confusingly, the ladder state on disk does not accurately reflect that an auto-write attempt committed real side effects.
recommendation: Either (a) treat saveState failure on rung-3 as a partial success — log the failure but return `autoVerifyResult` with a synthetic error explaining that snapshot+overwrite committed but persistence did not; or (b) on saveState rejection attempt to roll back target_file from the snapshot before returning. (a) is closer to existing semantics and matches verifySpot.ts's `pass: true, advanced: false, error: …` precedent.

title: Rung-3 auto-write writes payload with unresolved {{ ... }} placeholders when personalization key is missing
description: substitutePromptOnly intentionally leaves unknown `{{ key }}` placeholders intact (personalization.ts line 137 returns `_match`). Rung-3 reads auto.md, calls substitutePromptOnly with `state.personalization`, then passes the result to runAutoWrite which writes those bytes verbatim into target_file. If the student never called setPersonalization (or the path's personalization_options grew between cycles), payload retains literal `{{ pool_subset }}` substrings. Auto-write succeeds; pnpm build then fails with a syntax error in the user's `.tsx` source. The student gets a build failure instead of a structured `personalization-missing` error, and the snapshot rotation has already consumed one .bak slot.
impact: Silent failure in a load-bearing path: the user's source file is overwritten with unresolved templating, target_file is corrupt, and the failure surfaces as a downstream build error rather than as `requestHint`'s structured failure. Contract A10 requires substitution scope discipline but does not require pre-write validation; the silent placeholder retention is a real foot-gun on the rung-3 surface.
recommendation: Before calling runAutoWrite on rung 3, scan the substituted payload for any remaining `\{\{\s*[a-zA-Z_]` matches and short-circuit with a new `personalization-missing` error kind that names the unresolved key. Rung 1 / rung 2 paths can keep the leave-intact behavior (the payload is rendered as documentation, not committed to source).

title: runAutoWrite race window between fsPromises.access and fsPromises.rename swallows ENOENT data loss
description: Rotation uses access() to test whether `.bak` exists, then rename() to move it. If a concurrent process (or the user) deletes `.bak` between the two calls, rename rejects with ENOENT — which the catch block silently treats as 'no rotation needed' (`if (e.code !== 'ENOENT') throw …`). The same ENOENT classification covers two distinct cases: (1) `.bak` never existed (legitimate first-call path), (2) `.bak` was deleted between access and rename (a race that arguably should still try to write the new snapshot, which it does — but should surface a warning).
impact: Mostly cosmetic — the user gets a fresh snapshot either way. The concern is that the silent ENOENT swallow on rename masks fs-level corruption signals. Probability is low because no other process should touch `.sui-deepbook-course/snapshots/` during a rung-3 invocation.
recommendation: Use a single-step `fsPromises.rename(bakPath, rotatedPath).catch(err => { if (err.code === 'ENOENT') return; throw new AutoWriteError('snapshot-write-failed', …); })` and drop the access() probe. The TOCTOU is closed and the catch is one-step.

title: Rung-3 saveState failure does not roll back the target_file overwrite
description: When saveState rejects after a successful runAutoWrite, the snapshot file (`.bak`) holds the original bytes and target_file holds the new bytes — but the engine makes no attempt to roll target_file back to the snapshot before returning state-save-failed. The student is left with their source modified to the auto.md payload, with no surfaced affordance that recovery requires copying `.bak` back over target_file. Contract Behavior says "a crash mid-overwrite leaves both the snapshot and the (partially written) target_file recoverable by hand" — but a saveState failure is a process-level failure, not a crash, and the engine can synchronously roll back the writeFile.
impact: After a saveState rejection, the student sees their source mutated, no cursor advance, and a vague `state-save-failed` error — they may not realize that a recoverable `.bak` exists. Most likely class of failure (EACCES on .sui-deepbook-course because of parent-dir perms, ENOSPC) is exactly the case where the user is least equipped to recover by hand.
recommendation: On saveState rejection in the rung-3 path, attempt to copy `.bak` back over target_file before returning. If the rollback also fails, surface a richer error message naming both file paths so the user can recover manually. The atomicity claim in the contract is best-effort across snapshot+overwrite, but saveState failure is a synchronously-recoverable case.

title: Rung-3 auto-write-failed catch loses original AutoWriteError.kind detail in message
description: When runAutoWrite rejects with AutoWriteError, requestHint maps every sub-kind ('target-file-missing', 'target-range-invalid', 'snapshot-write-failed', 'overwrite-failed') into a single 'auto-write-failed' error kind with the sub-kind embedded only in the human-readable message. AutoWriteError.kind is the existing structured discriminator; collapsing it loses programmatic recovery affordances (e.g. the agent cannot tell apart "path data is wrong" from "disk is full" without parsing the message string).
impact: Reduced debuggability and harder remediation logic for the conductor agent: it has to regex-parse the message to know whether to ask the user to free space, fix the manifest, or report a config bug. Contract A7 distinguishes the four AutoWriteErrorKind values for a reason.
recommendation: Add an optional `subKind?: AutoWriteErrorKind` to RequestHintError and forward `err.kind` when err is an AutoWriteError. The discriminator stays at `'auto-write-failed'` for compatibility but the granular kind is reachable by callers.

title: runAutoWrite ENOENT vs other read errors collapsed into target-file-missing
description: The target_file read catch maps every error to AutoWriteError 'target-file-missing', explicitly handling ENOENT but then falling through to the same kind for other ENXIO/EACCES/EISDIR codes. The message changes ('Target file not found' vs 'Failed to read target file') but the discriminator is identical. EACCES and EISDIR are categorically different from a missing file and should not classify as 'missing'.
impact: Misleading error classification. A student who has a permission misconfiguration on src/App.tsx gets an 'auto-write-failed (target-file-missing)' message that suggests they should create the file, when in fact they need to chmod it.
recommendation: Introduce a 'target-file-unreadable' kind distinct from 'target-file-missing' (or rename the kind to 'target-file-unreadable'), and only use 'target-file-missing' when err.code === 'ENOENT'. AutoWriteErrorKind already carries the discriminator.

title: AbortSignal.timeout(5000) on checkManifest does not narrow the AbortError vs other rejection — silent classification
description: checkManifest catches all fetch rejections (including the AbortError fired by AbortSignal.timeout) and returns false. The probe surface is correct (timeout → false → probe fails with a remediable shell action), but the error is fully discarded — the function doesn't even log which class of failure occurred (timeout vs DNS vs ECONNREFUSED). Operators investigating a flaky preflight cannot distinguish 'manifest server is down' from 'manifest server is hung past 5s' from inside the probe.
impact: Diagnostic gap, not a correctness bug. Cycle 3 R3-005 was about adding the timeout (which is done correctly); the silent collapse is the long-tail concern.
recommendation: Optionally log to stderr via console.warn() when err.name === 'TimeoutError' (the AbortSignal.timeout rejection class) so operators can distinguish hung-server from down-server. Probe contract stays unchanged.

title: parseCommand silently strips unbalanced double quotes — tokenizes garbage instead of rejecting
description: When a double-quoted segment is opened but never closed (e.g. `pnpm "build` with no closing quote), the inner loop runs to end-of-string and the `if (i < s.length) i++` step is silently skipped. The result is that everything from the opening quote to EOL becomes a single arg. There is no rejection of malformed quoting. A curriculum author writing `pnpm "build dir` (typo) gets a silently-mangled spawn argv rather than a parse error.
impact: Silent garbage-in / garbage-out. The cycle-4 L001 carry-forward is about respecting quotes; an unbalanced quote should be a parse error, not a tokenization heuristic. Probability of a real curriculum author hitting this is low (build_command is usually 'pnpm build') but the failure mode is exactly the kind of thing that would make a manifest-author's day hard to debug because the spawn just fails with 'command not found' or similar.
recommendation: Add a balance-tracking flag inside the quote-reading loop and throw `parseCommand: unbalanced double quote in '<input>'` when EOL is hit before the closing quote. Mirrors the empty-string rejection at line 84.
